topic: weather forecast and impact on honey flow

turns.content: I wonder whether you might help me think through something. The forecast for the next fortnight is showing quite warm temperatures — high twenties — but very little rain. I am trying to work out what that means for the honey flow.

That's a really important question for this time of year. Hot, dry weather has a complicated relationship with honey flow. The warmth itself is good — bees fly more and forage longer in warm conditions. But the lack of rain is the concern. Without moisture, many nectar-producing plants slow down or stop producing nectar entirely. What are your main nectar sources at the moment? That will make a big difference in how the dry spell affects things.

The main sources at this time of year are the field beans in the neighbouring farm, the lime trees along the village lane, and the clover in the meadow behind the house. The limes are usually the big producers in late June and July.

That's a useful mix. The lime trees should be fairly resilient — they have deep root systems, so a couple of dry weeks won't shut down their nectar production the way it would for shallow-rooted plants. Lime honey flow tends to be driven more by temperature and humidity than by rainfall, and warm weather with some overnight humidity can produce excellent nectar secretion from limes. The clover is more vulnerable — it's shallow-rooted and will slow down quickly without rain. White clover especially tends to stop producing nectar when the soil dries out. And the field beans — are they still flowering, or are they coming to the end?

The beans are nearly finished, I should think. The farmer planted them in February and they have been flowering for several weeks now. I expect another week at most. So it is really the limes and the clover I am thinking about.

Right, so the beans are winding down naturally regardless of the weather. For the next fortnight, I'd expect the limes to be your main producer. The warm temperatures should actually boost the lime flow — limes often produce best when daytime temperatures are in the mid-to-high twenties and nights stay above about twelve degrees. If you're getting those conditions, the bees could bring in a good amount. The clover will likely tail off as the soil dries. Have you checked the supers recently? Are the bees bringing in well at the moment?

I checked on Saturday and they were drawing comb on the second super quite enthusiastically, which is encouraging. The first super is about three-quarters capped. I was wondering whether I ought to add a third, in case the lime flow is strong, or whether that would be premature.

If the first is three-quarters capped and they're actively drawing the second, I'd lean toward adding the third sooner rather than later. The general rule is to add space before they need it rather than after — if a strong lime flow comes in and they run out of room, you risk triggering swarming behaviour or having them pack the brood nest with honey, which creates its own problems. Better to have an empty super on top that they don't quite fill than to be caught short during a strong flow. You can always consolidate later.

That is what I was thinking, but I wanted to hear it confirmed. I have spare drawn comb from last year, which should help them get started on a third super more quickly than foundation would. I shall add it this week, I think. The other question is whether I ought to be providing water. The stream at the bottom of the garden has gone rather low.

Drawn comb is perfect — they'll fill that much faster than they'd draw fresh foundation, which means you won't lose any of the flow to wax production. As for water, yes, I'd definitely set something up. Bees need a lot of water in hot weather, both for cooling the hive and for diluting stored honey to feed brood. If the stream is low, they'll go looking elsewhere, and that usually means neighbours' birdbaths or swimming pools, which doesn't tend to make one popular. A shallow dish with pebbles or corks for landing spots, kept topped up, placed somewhere shady — that should do it. Keep it in the same spot so they learn where it is.

I have a shallow terracotta saucer that Margaret used under one of her pots. That would do rather well, I should think. I shall fill it with some stones and set it near the hives. Thank you — this has been most useful. I feel rather more prepared for the warm spell now.

That sounds ideal — terracotta is good because it stays cool and the rough surface gives the bees grip. Just keep it topped up daily in the heat. With the third super ready to go, drawn comb in hand, and a water station sorted, you're well set up. If the lime flow comes in strong, you could have a very good July. Enjoy the warm weather.
